aesthetic: The Transparent Circle — a Concord engraving of the Over-Soul
anchor: Emerson's essay 'Circles' (1841) and the Over-Soul read as concentric emanation from the transparent eyeball, set in the type and paper of James Munroe & Co.'s Boston first editions and The Dial (1840-44): granite gray, foxed-paper cream, Walden pond-light, New England autumn russet, harvest/Persian gilt, and the indigo of the 'currents of the Universal Being.'
rationale: The corpus's structural metaphor is the concentric circle — 'The eye is the first circle; the horizon which it forms is the second; and throughout nature this primary figure is repeated without end' ('Circles', 1841) — and the Over-Soul within which 'every man's particular being is contained.' Its primary artifacts are the 1840s Boston essay/lecture in Munroe & Co. and Dial type, the transparent-eyeball passage of Nature, and the New England landscape of Concord and Walden. So the world is cool, expansive, nature-flooded and engraved — pond-and-sky teal, pine, russet October, twilight indigo over granite and foxed paper — deliberately the opposite pole from the warm, spare, bone-pale graphite of the Dickinson 'Fascicle Leaf' next door in Massachusetts: a teal accent against her amber, a sturdy Georgia/old-style display against her Baskerville, circles and emanation against her dash and negative space.

fonts.display: 'Iowan Old Style', 'Palatino Linotype', Palatino, Georgia, serif
fonts.body: Georgia, 'Iowan Old Style', 'Times New Roman', serif
